article_title: I Still Run These Docker Containers Every Day in 2026 (After Moving to Kubernetes)
core_thesis: After migrating from Docker Swarm to Kubernetes (Talos Linux with GitOps), the author intentionally maintains a hybrid home lab where certain infrastructure tools remain as Docker containers. Kubernetes is reserved for stateless, scalable, GitOps-driven workloads, while Docker handles single-instance utilities, monitoring, and management tools that benefit from simplicity, faster troubleshooting, and independence from the cluster. The article argues that a 'one-size-fits-all' approach to container orchestration is suboptimal; instead, workload characteristics should dictate the platform.
detailed_summary: The author, Brandon Lee, describes his hybrid home lab architecture after moving production workloads to Kubernetes. He runs Proxmox as the hypervisor, Ceph for distributed storage, Talos Linux Kubernetes for application workloads, and Docker for infrastructure tools. He emphasizes that Kubernetes is overkill for single-instance tools, infrastructure utilities, monitoring sidecars, admin dashboards, and services that rarely change. For these, Docker provides faster deployments, easier troubleshooting, and lower overhead. He lists nine Docker containers he still runs daily: Kasm (browser-based workspaces), Loggifly (log monitoring with keyword alerts), Sidero Omni (Talos cluster management – kept outside the cluster it manages for DR), Pulse (Proxmox monitoring), Netdata (real-time system monitoring), Nginx Proxy Manager (reverse proxy with UI), Portainer/Komodo (Docker management UIs), Dozzle (real-time container log viewer), and Traefik (reverse proxy used both in Docker and Kubernetes). He explains why each stays in Docker, often citing simplicity, lack of need for clustering, and the principle that management tools should not depend on the cluster they manage. The hybrid model balances flexibility and simplicity: Kubernetes for scalable, declarative, GitOps-driven apps; Docker for fast, independent, infrastructure-focused tools.

key_data_points: The author migrated from Docker Swarm to Kubernetes (Talos Linux + GitOps) but intentionally kept a hybrid model: Kubernetes for scalable, stateless, GitOps-driven apps; Docker for single-instance infrastructure tools. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab/DevOps: provides a practical architecture pattern for balancing complexity and simplicity.)

Kubernetes is considered overkill for single-instance tools, infrastructure utilities, monitoring sidecars, admin dashboards, and services that rarely change. Docker offers faster deployments, easier troubleshooting, and lower overhead for these workloads. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab: helps you decide which workloads to containerize vs. orchestrate.)

Sidero Omni is run as a Docker container outside the Kubernetes cluster it manages to ensure management plane availability even if the cluster fails. The author emphasizes that management tools should not depend on the cluster they manage. (🟢 Relevant to your Red Teaming: this is a resilience pattern – if you compromise the Docker host running Omni, you could gain control over the entire Kubernetes cluster.)

Kasm provides browser-based isolated workspaces and is used for testing applications, accessing tools remotely, and opening questionable links in disposable sessions. (🟢 Relevant to your Red Teaming: can be used as a safe sandbox for analyzing malware or testing exploits without contaminating your main environment.)

Loggifly monitors Docker container logs for keywords like 'error' or 'failed' and sends notifications. It is lightweight and purpose-built for Docker. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab/DevOps: useful for proactive monitoring and alerting without heavy tooling.)

Netdata provides near-instant real-time system metrics with no complex configuration, making it ideal for troubleshooting. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab: quick visibility into system health during incidents.)

Nginx Proxy Manager (NPM) is kept in Docker because it is extremely easy to manage via UI, great for non-Kubernetes services, and fast to update. The author notes that Traefik is a better fit for infrastructure-as-code enthusiasts. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab: choose NPM for simplicity, Traefik for IaC.)

Portainer and Komodo provide fast visibility into Docker containers and stacks, useful for troubleshooting and managing updates across multiple Docker hosts. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab: simplifies container management without CLI.)
Dozzle is a lightweight, zero-configuration web interface for viewing Docker container logs in real time. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab/DevOps: essential for quick log inspection without SSH.)

Traefik is used both in Docker and Kubernetes, providing consistent configuration between environments. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab: if you use Traefik in Kubernetes, you can reuse the same config for Docker services.)
methodology_steps: Assess workload characteristics: classify apps as stateless/scalable/GitOps-driven (Kubernetes candidates) vs. single-instance/infrastructure/monitoring/admin (Docker candidates).
Set up Proxmox as the hypervisor, Ceph for distributed storage, and Talos Linux for Kubernetes.
Deploy Kubernetes for application workloads that benefit from scalability, automation, and declarative GitOps (e.g., using ArgoCD).
Keep a separate Docker host (or VM) for infrastructure tools: Kasm, Loggifly, Sidero Omni, Pulse, Netdata, Nginx Proxy Manager, Portainer/Komodo, Dozzle, and Traefik.
For each Docker container, use simple docker run or docker-compose commands; avoid adding Kubernetes manifests for these tools.
Ensure management tools (like Sidero Omni) are isolated from the cluster they manage to maintain availability during cluster outages.
Use Nginx Proxy Manager (or Traefik) as a central reverse proxy for Docker-based services; manage SSL certificates via the UI or Let's Encrypt.
Monitor Docker containers with Loggifly (keyword alerts) and Netdata (real-time metrics); use Dozzle for log viewing.
Manage Docker containers via Portainer or Komodo for visual oversight and quick restarts.
Maintain the hybrid model: Kubernetes for 'production' apps, Docker for utilities; evaluate each new tool on a case-by-case basis.
conclusions: A hybrid Docker + Kubernetes approach is often more practical than an all-or-nothing migration. The author found that keeping certain infrastructure tools in Docker reduces complexity and improves troubleshooting speed, while Kubernetes handles scalable, GitOps-driven workloads. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab/DevOps: adopt a similar hybrid model to avoid over-engineering simple services.)

Management tools should be decoupled from the cluster they manage. Running Sidero Omni outside the Kubernetes cluster ensures that the management plane remains available even if the cluster fails. (🟢 Relevant to your Red Teaming: this decoupling creates an external attack surface – compromising the Docker host with Omni could yield cluster admin access.)

Tools like Kasm provide disposable, isolated environments ideal for security testing. The author uses Kasm to open questionable links in isolated sessions, which aligns with safe browsing and malware analysis practices. (🟢 Relevant to your Red Teaming: integrate Kasm into your workflow for safe execution of untrusted code or phishing analysis.)

Simplicity wins for infrastructure utilities. Netdata, Dozzle, and Loggifly are lightweight, zero-configuration tools that provide immediate value without the overhead of Prometheus/Grafana stacks. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab: start with these before investing in heavy monitoring solutions.)
personal_relevance_summary: This article directly supports your homelab and DevOps interests by providing a concrete, real-world hybrid architecture that balances Kubernetes power with Docker simplicity. You can apply the same decision framework to your own lab: reserve Kubernetes for scalable, GitOps-driven apps, and keep infrastructure tools (monitoring, proxies, management UIs) in Docker for faster iteration and easier troubleshooting. For your Red Teaming practice, the article highlights valuable tools like Kasm (isolated browsing/analysis) and the security implication of decoupling management planes (e.g., Sidero Omni outside the cluster). The detailed list of Docker containers with deployment rationales gives you a ready-made toolkit to enhance your lab's monitoring, logging, and management capabilities. As a student, the article's structured approach to evaluating workload placement can inform your own system design projects.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a dedicated Docker host VM (or lightweight LXC) for all infrastructure tools listed. Use Ansible or a simple shell script to bootstrap the entire stack in minutes. For Red Teaming, deploy Kasm with custom workspaces containing penetration testing tools (e.g., Kali Linux container) to create disposable, isolated attack environments. Also, experiment with running Sidero Omni in Docker to manage a Talos Kubernetes cluster – this gives you a realistic management-plane separation pattern that you can later test for resilience or attack scenarios.
